summary: Four-file overlap between sibling Phase 4 rows (settings.json, todos.yaml, internal/cli/app.go, schemas/blocker-taxonomy.yaml) is a row-scope-too-broad signal: when two parallel rows both touch the CLI surface, the global hook config, the shared blocker registry, and the shared todos almanac, the supposedly-disjoint scopes weren't disjoint.
detail: blocker-taxonomy-foundation and orchestration-delegation-contract were planned as parallel rows with non-overlapping deliverables, yet collided on four files: (1) .claude/settings.json — both edited the PreToolUse hook list (blocker removed gate-check; orchestration added layer-guard + new SendMessage matcher); (2) .furrow/almanac/todos.yaml — both rows discovered follow-ups and appended; (3) internal/cli/app.go — both added stdin to App and a NewWithStdin constructor with conflicting positional order; (4) schemas/blocker-taxonomy.yaml — both extended the registry. Three of four were trivially unionable because IDs/codes were disjoint, but app.go required real synthesis: orchestration's hook subcommand pattern won the architectural decision per cartographer Option A; both NewWithStdin constructors converged on (stdout, stderr, stdin); the top-level `guard` command was preserved alongside `hook` to keep blocker tests passing without a forced rewrite. Lesson: when sibling rows both touch shared registries (hook config, taxonomy, almanac) or shared surfaces (CLI app struct), declare the shared file as a coordination point in row definitions, not as 'incidental' file_ownership. The mid-merge hook-self-block during integration (furrow hook layer-guard refused .furrow/* bash arguments) was an additional friction signal: enforcement infrastructure landed before the integration tooling that has to coexist with it.
